title: The Macro Minute: Is it safe to buy the dip?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Monday,
March 16th, 2026. Hope everyone had a
great weekend. So, as always, we start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
is it safe to buy the dip? The answer
is, not likely.
The US-Israel war remains far from
resolved, and as we discussed last week,
global liquidity will likely continue to
ebb for as long as it persists.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle. In the US, the January
JOLTS, February industrial production,
and March University of Michigan
consumer sentiment survey data were
generally supportive of our grid model
projections of accelerating growth and
supportive of our grid model projections
of slowing inflation here in the US.
The weak negative impulse in private
sector job openings in the January JOLTS
data was supportive of our jobless
recovery thesis. Elsewhere, the strong
positive impulses in industrial
production and capacity utilization to
well above trend rates in February were
supportive of our paradigm C theme.
Looking abroad, in China, the January
through February activity uh data and
property supply data were generally
supportive of our outlook for firming
growth. China's February property demand
data, however, were unsupportive of our
outlook for firming growth in China.
Transition to our inflation uh cycle to
the inflation cycle.
Persistent below trend rates of private
sector hiring, quits, and layoffs and
discharges in the January JOLTS data
were supportive of our continued
disinflation, driven primarily by
housing and labor thesis. Reminder,
workers who remain at their jobs tend to
experience much slower wage growth than
workers who change jobs. If AI diffusion
plus a jobless recovery equals a
structural uptrend in productivity
growth and a structural downtrend in
wage growth, then US inflation is
unlikely to durably misbehave absent a
sustained energy price shock.
Elsewhere, the fifth consecutive
month-over-month decline in the
political party adjusted one-year median
CPI expectation in the March University
of Michigan consumer sentiment survey
data was supportive of our continued
disinflation driven primarily by housing
and labor thesis. In China, the January
through February activity and property
prices data were generally supportive of
our outlook for slowing inflation. With
regards to the monetary policy and
fiscal policy cycles, nothing actionable
with respect to our Bayesian priors
regarding the liquidity cycle, the risk
appetite strengthened as energy prices
declined reflecting investor optimism
that a greater number of tankers may
soon transit the Strait of Hormuz. Even
as vessel movement through the strategic
corridor remained close to frozen, US
crude declined to roughly $95 a barrel
as limit as a limited flow of tankers
cautiously resumed passage. With the war
in Iran entering its third week,
President Trump intensified diplomatic
pressure on global governments to assist
in restoring access to the maritime
choke point by deploying naval assets to
escort commercial shipping.
NATO confirmed that quote individual
allies are talking with the US and
others on what more they might do.
Thus far, no government has indicated a
willingness to commit warships to the
mission. Elsewhere, US Treasury
Secretary Scott Bessent told you at CNBC
that the US is not intervening in energy
derivatives markets despite crude
futures surging to nearly four-year
highs amid the conflict adding that
quote prices will be much
probably much lower than $80 a barrel
within a couple of months.
His commentary suggests the
administration anticipates this conflict
will drag on for at least a few more
weeks.
All told, the US-Israel-Iran war remains
far from resolved and as we discussed
last week, global liquidity will likely
continue to add for as long as it
persists.
Continue reviewing to learn whether our
institutional grade risk management
overlays Kissin and Doctor Mo are
evolving their signals in response to
these or anticipated fundamental
dynamics.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard as
always a wrap up with a question from
our community. Not necessarily a
question, but since you know, we're
getting a lot of positive feedback on
our most recent a scanning report
presentation, I thought we could with
this uh brief comment here, which is uh
titled MSR. Uh "Skipper, you always say
that it's the best MSR ever, but I
always have I have to say uh this is
really true, especially for this one. Uh
in fact, would you consider making it
public because this has um this has
critical research that the whole world
should hear and hopefully make people
aware of what they vote for. Uh I'm
grateful uh to be on your team. Uh so,
um just thank you for the super kind
feedback. Uh for those maybe unfamiliar,
we do uh you know, our kind of our our
our research process is organized. Our
daily lead-off morning notes is our
Bayesian inference process where we, you
know, analyze data, analyze policy
signals, and ultimately analyze uh
market movements through the lens of our
our our market regime nowcasting process
and all the tools that go that feed into
that system. Uh it's where we analyze
the things and and sort of help
investors understand how the
distribution of probable economic policy
market outcomes is evolving in a way
that gives them enough confidence about
both sides of the distribution in terms
of what's likely to transpire so that
they can dispassionately execute our
KISS and Dr. Mo's signals whenever they
uh pivot from, you know, pricing in, you
know, let's say right tail risk to left
tail risk or vice versa. You know,
that's what we're trying to do here.
We're not trying to bludgeon them with
bullish information where bullish or
bludgeon them with bearish information
where bearish like most research
providers do. That's not what
institutional investors do. This is an
institutional investment product and
service. We have many of the top funds
across global Wall Street, all who are
paying for our research. And so, you
know, we're not going to get in
embarrass ourselves in front of them.
And so, by bludgeoning people over the
head with bulls, you know, bull porn and
and bear porn. That's not That's not
what institutional investors do. So, uh
how our research process is organized is
again, you know, trying to understand
what's evolving across these six key
macro cycles from a Bayesian inference
perspective. And then on Saturday in our
Around the Horn webcast, we sort of
unpack what we think are the most
important things that our broadest
audience should should be aware of
uh from that uh uh
uh most recent week. And then every
month in our monthly macro scouting
reports, that's us doing the deep dive,
you know, data-driven, uh robustly
researched, you know, kind of research
in order to figure out what's likely to
transpire in terms of the growth,
inflation, monetary policy, fiscal
policy, and liquidity cycles, and
positioning cycles on a medium-term time
horizon, so that investors can have that
sort of like our, you know, if you think
about our our research process, you
know, the lead-off morning note is us
pointing the flashlight down where our
feet are.
The round the horn is sort of us, you
know, turning around and saying, "Hey,
this is all the most important." Taking
our flashlight and saying, "Hey, these
are all the things that we just flashed
a light on and said, 'Hey, this is the
stuff that matters the most to you
staying on the right side of market risk
as an investor.'" And then our monthly
macro scouting report is us taking our
flashlight, screwing on some sort of
like, you know,
you know, extender of the of the beam,
so that we can shine as far as a beam
into this very dark, uncertain future as
we possibly can across those six key
macro cycles. And so that, you know,
investors can, you know, have a really
good, you know, fundamental basis for
what they think is likely to transpire
in the economy and asset markets, and
ultimately how policy is going to
perpetuate and or respond to that. And
so that's all, you know, we do
fundamental research here at 42 Macro
not to tell investors what to do in
their portfolio with. Zero percent of
our fundamental research ever impacts
our client portfolios. We are very
explicit, and I'm very explicit in my
own portfolio, and very explicit with
our customers that KISS and Doctor Mo,
our quantitative risk management
overlays, which are being employed by
thousands of investors in 80 countries
around the world and many of the top
funds across global Wall Street, those
are what should to tell you to do in
your portfolio. And we're really
grateful that we have so many wonderful
partners, both retail and professional
investors all around the world who have
recognized that and are partnered with
us for that. So, we're wrapping up
there. Darius Dale here, representing
our macro minute for Monday, March 16th,
2026. Best of luck out there today.
We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
